speaker: interviewer
text: When is your wedding? In June? Your anniversary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually it was May, June, and twice we went under the chuppah. This is also another story. It's another story. Because, because in Montreal, Quebec, only the religious wedding counts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Should I shut up?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. That's OK. I'm rolling bars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. And because she was under age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they needed the permission of the parents. In the meantime, I didn't have money. I needed a room to live together. So I told the rabbi first make the chuppah. He didn't want to do it because it's against the law only. So finally--

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 19th, 1995. Interview conducted with Ernest Rubin, interviewer, Rickey Halperin, Brooklyn, New York. Interview conducted in English. I said it. Please state your full name, including your Hebrew name and any nicknames, and your date of birth and age.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ernest Rubin. In Hebrew, they call me Chaim Tzvi. And in Yiddish, they used to call me Hershel. Was born in 1928, December 6.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little town-- Somotor in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near any big towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: The big town is the Hungarian town, Satoraljaujhely. That's where the ghetto was. But the biggest town now in Slovakia is Kosice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me something about your family life, first beginning with how many people were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother, she had 10 children. I knew seven-- four brothers and three sisters. And I had a brother. He was 13 years old when he died. He had an appendix infection that he get into the hospital. He died. And then I had a little brother. I think I remember-- I must be maybe three years old. I think he died. He had a circumcision. And it was, it was no good. And he bleed to death. And I think there was a other little baby born dead, the way I am informed. But this was all before I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other people living together with you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my grandfather and grandmother from my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about life in Somotor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, life in Somotor, like any-- if you saw this was exactly life in Somotor. A little boy, I was three years old, they cut my hair off, I live pious, and I was going to cheder until six years old. And when I was six years old, I was going to school in the morning. And after school, I was going back to cheder. And it was life in the little town. That's all I knew. I didn't go nowhere. And my father, they had the little business of town-- little town tavern. And he was working in the business. And my grandfather was helping out. But mainly, my father and grandfather, they were studying the Talmud. I know my father used to get up 3 o'clock in the morning, prepare the tea and milk for my grandfather, and making warm the room because we didn't have gas heat like this. We had the little ovens with coal. He used to heat up the room when he gets up, my grandfather, room should be comfortable. And 4 o'clock already, my grandfather was studying the Talmud. And my father, he went into his room, studying the Talmud till, till the morning. And then they went to shul to pray. And we had the little shul for the week. Our second house in the backyard, the old house, we made a little what they call [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That's where-- and that was life. And my mother took care of the house. We had a cow. We had chickens. We had geese. And you know, like a household, and she took care of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between the non-Jews and the Jews in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the town, actually, the relationship was not bad those days. This I'm talking about before 1938. Because actually, we had lots of non-Jewish friends. And they knew the family, because everybody used to come to drink in the tavern. All the high class people, of course, after two hours being there, they were all drunk. But everybody liked the family, you know? They had a very good name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what a Shabbos was like in the summertime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos-- Thursday morning, my mother will get up 2 o'clock in the morning. She will bake her own bread because we had the oven in the kitchen-- the challah. I remember one bread used to be big, almost like a tire for a car because it was ten people in the house. When you started to cut one bread, the evening, it was gone. And the challah and, and the cakes, you know, that used to be a big thing in, in our house. And Friday night, we used to go to shul. And Shabbos, Saturday morning, we used to go into the village because we had to walk two kilometers. So we went in there. And then we come home. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that's what it used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it that there were guests at the Shabbos table?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there used to. There was a tradition if a poor man came, for example, to the town, we used to invite them to the table. That was a tradition. And there was always two beds in the small synagogue. It was in the back of our house-- always two beds there in case a poor man gets stuck for Shabbos, he should have where to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house, we spoke Yiddish. On the street, we spoke Hungarian. In school, when I started to go to school when I was six years old, was Czechoslovakian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your classmates in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of the Jewish kids, they went to the Czechoslovakian school. So they were mixed-- non-Jews and Jews. It didn't matter. There was no problem till 1938. There was no-- everything was under control on the, the Czechoslovakian Republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a spiritual leader in the town that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Our neighbor was Chaim Freidman. He was the rabbi, the shohet. And he used to come Saturday in school to teach us extra. It was a law that all the Jewish kids had to go Saturday afternoon to school. And he was teaching us what Jewish religion. But of course, we knew already everything because in the cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was, you know, the Christian children, they went to the church in the middle of the week to learn religion. And we had to do it Saturday afternoon. And Chaim Friedman, he used to teach us, to explain, you know? What about holidays, Jewish holidays, like Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that celebrated in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we used to bake our own matzos. And it was a big thing. It was our neighbor Cornfeld. That's where we used to bake the matzos for Pesach. Because mostly shmurah matzos-- you probably know what shmurah matzos is. My father, my grandfather wouldn't eat other matzos, just shmurah matzos. And we used to bake it. And I used to go to help. And I used to carry it into the oven and put it together in a-- And the Seder, of course, it was a tradition with my father, my grandfather. It was a big table, at least 15 people there. Because my sister will come home or my brother-in-law will come to Somotor to sit. Or somebody, the relatives will come. It was a big treat, actually, the, the Pesach Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there also a cultural life-- theaters, books, newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. No, it was too small of a town. And we didn't go to theaters. Our job was to study. That's the only thing we had to do. We couldn't go-- once in awhile, I used to sneak out to play soccer with the other kids. That's all. It's also just by stealing the time. Nobody should know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler rose to power in 1933, did Nazism mean anything to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- I was only, what, four years old. But we started to hear already from the anti-Semites when I went to school, Hitler is coming. You know, they start to give us the needle, but we didn't realize that really, it's going to be so serious. Because I think in 1936, I mentioned to you the president of Czechoslovakia, Dr. Tomas Garrigue Masaryk-- I show you the picture-- he died. And we had a radio. Those days, we had no electricity. The radio went on a battery. And I saw my grandfather listening to the news that he died. And I see my grandfather crying. And he said, no, our problem will start. So that, I remember. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say a young boy would have imagined for himself, his future, like in a town that you grow up-- grew up in before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the truth as a kid, they used to tell me-- my father and mother-- if you don't study the Talmud, you will become a shoemaker or a tailor. It was to make me scared in order I should study. So everything was, you know, they will die from hunger, but everybody had to go to yeshiva, all four boys. And my brothers, they were already in the yeshiva. They were much older than me. Everybody went at least till 20, 21, to the Yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And usually they went out of your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes out of the town in-- because you didn't have so much money to pay for the yeshiva. So what happened, Jewish families used to take you in. Let's say Monday, I was eating by this family, Tuesday another family or people who knew our family or a little bit related. So like this, I finished the week every day by-- they used to call it [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE], So that's what we used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Shabbos, you came back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. No. Not-- it's depend where. When I was close already to Somotor in the later years, in 1943, '44, I used to come home for Shabbat. Because it was only seven miles by train. It was only 20 minutes. But when I used to study-- in, in 1938, when Czechoslovakia was liquidated, the part where I was born, they gave it to the Hungarians. So my mother, let her rest in peace, she was born in Hungary. I mention to you, [INAUDIBLE]. You will probably have a problem to pronounce it. And all the education problem for me and for my brother, you know, who, unfortunately, didn't came back, Betzalel Josef, they send us to [INAUDIBLE]. Because there, from my mother's side, the whole family was there, my grandparents. My mother had four brothers there. And they were quite wealthy. They had a flour mill. And my sister was married there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was sleeping in my sister's place. She had a business, you know, a textile business. And every day, I went to my uncle to eat-- two days by this uncle, two days by other uncle. That's the way we used to solve the problem. Would you like to mention the name of your grandparents from--

speaker: interviewer
text: From my mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandfather, I never knew because he died before I was born. And his name was Dovid Lieb. Kohn David was his-- But I remember very, very well my grandmother. And my grandmother's maiden name was Weinberger. And they had-- she had a brother. [INAUDIBLE] Moshe we used to call him. And he had the flour mill in [INAUDIBLE]. And my grandfather had 25% from the mill. And I know, because I was a child, the 25% was divided between my four uncles and my mother. So we had-- we owned 5% of the mill. Actually, we made a living from this, not from the tavern what we had. Because the tavern, you know, when the Hungarians came in, they, they came into the town. They saw the sign, Rubin Tavern. Right away, we had to take down the sign in the door to be closed. We are not supposed to sell. They took away the liquor license right away. We had no business the first day they come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hungary acquired Slovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, that's right, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other changes did you feel when that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Felt anti-Semitism started already in 1938. But we were still-- they didn't-- they let us alone, the Hungarians. The only thing, they took the business away. We had to close the business. But when you were traveling in the train, let's say my father, he was a religious man with a beard, he had already problem. They're going to call him, you dirty Jew. We're going to walk on the street, you dirty Jew. So it start already. In '39, you know, it's already, and every year, more and more. In 1940, I think, already, they-- my brother, who died here two months ago, and my other brother, David, they took him right away into the labor camp to work for the Germans, you know, digging the ditches. And the way the Germans were going to Russia, they were almost near Moscow. And they used the Jewish labor to do all kinds of slave labor for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying they were drafted into the Hungarian labor force?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. First, they took him into the army. They gave him a regular Hungarian army uniform. And then later, they were not supposed to wear the uniform. They had to wear their own clothing with a yellow band to show that they are in the labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did refugees arrive from, like, Poland into your town, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is when Germany attacked Poland. The army-- I don't know how-- they were running through our little town. And I remember-- I don't remember. It was '39 maybe. It was a big snowstorm, maybe six feet of snow. And they-- I remember the Polish soldiers, they were frozen to death under the snow. Because they, they didn't know where they are running. They were running from the Germans. But Poland had a treaty with Hungarian, friendship treaty. And Hungary permitted the Polish army to go through. Eventually, I think they end up in England, I think, or in Russia, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there Jewish refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just army-- we didn't know about the Jews, nothing, just because they were all in military.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were no Jewish refugees who had succeeded in escaping once the Nazis starting coming into power?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- we didn't know even that the Polish Jew-- when they took us to the ghetto, we didn't even know that the Polish Jew was liquidated-- three and a half million Jews. We didn't know. The only thing I remember, you know, was getting-- you know, we are jumping back and forth through the story. Every time when I used to study in [INAUDIBLE], the seven kilometers-- the seven miles-- from Somotor, the rabbi used to ask me all these questions. Because I was always interested in politics, even as a kid. Every time they were talking in shul after davening, after praying, they said, Roosevelt. Roosevelt, Churchill will save us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in my mind-- you know, I didn't know how serious it is. In my mind, I was sure Roosevelt, President Roosevelt and Churchill will save, save us. It never happened. Unfortunately, I had to come here to find out that President Roosevelt was an anti-Semite. But this is another story for another day. Tell me about your bar mitzvah, 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: '41 or '42, something like this. My bar mitzvah was in [INAUDIBLE] because then-- because I used to come home once a year only. Because it was hard already. You know, you had to feed another person. So they needed me in [INAUDIBLE]. I had to go to cheder, to yeshiva, in the morning, 5 o'clock in the morning till 7:00, yeshiva. 7:00, go to shul, you go round to my uncle to eat breakfast. 8 o'clock, we are to go to school. The school was a private school. Was there until 1 o'clock. 1 o'clock, we had to run [BACKROUND NOISE] to eat lunch. You know, the lunch was the dinner. Like here, it's Dinner time. There, we used to eat lunch-- I mean, the big dinner was 1 o'clock in the afternoon. So sometimes I had to go two kilometers to my uncle where the meal was. Because there, I had two days I was eating there. Then I had to run back for 2 o'clock already to be back walking, because the yeshiva started 7 o'clock in the night. I didn't even have time to study, you know, because the Talmud. And I-- the Talmud with the schooling, the homework-- I just, I couldn't do it. It was too much for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the problem with my sister-- my brother-in-law was not home anymore. They took him to labor camp. And she had three little children. And I had to help in the store to watch they shouldn't steal the merchandise. If the merchandise arrive, I had to go with a bicycle to put the textile on the bicycle, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was heavy. I used to turn over. You know, it was so heavy to bring the textile, the merchandise, into the store and, and help her there. It was terrible. We didn't know where my brother-in-law is. Finally, my brother-in-law and his brother were shot to death in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is all during the year of your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- my bar mitzvah was in my brother-in-law's father's house. That's where my bar mitzvah was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents were not there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father only, because they came from Somotor. It was a big-- it was-- the whole trip was maybe 75 miles from Somotor to [INAUDIBLE]. But to go from Somotor to [INAUDIBLE], it took-- you had to take a train 4 o'clock in the morning to [INAUDIBLE], where the ghetto was. Then you had to wait for three hours. There was a little train. You know, it took forever. You went maybe 20 miles. You had to take another little train. You had to wait there maybe four or five hours. Sometime it took two days to get there. So it was very hard. But my father, I remember he was there. Because I remember even the speech I made there. But it was sad already. It was, it was painful, you know, because all my uncles, my cousins, they were all already-- they were older. And everybody was already in the labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then on march 19, 1944, the Germans come into Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-Was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 1944. Yeah. I just don't remember the date. Because I know that they let us-- Pesach-- they let us finish the holiday in the house. We were not supposed to leave the town anymore. We are not supposed to leave even the house. And I remember that, you know, right away after Pesach, they took us to the ghetto to Satoraljaujhely. That's where the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were at home that Pesach with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. This was-- that was-- the last year and a half, I was studying-- I was not studying anymore in, in [INAUDIBLE]. I was studying in the town that was 11 kilometers from us. Because, you know, somebody had to be close to the house. It was-- once there was in 1942 maybe-- in the middle of the studying in yeshiva in [INAUDIBLE]-- I'm jumping back to the story-- they-- there was a film showing. The name for the film, [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know if you ever heard about it. They showed-- it's a propaganda film that show the way the Jews are cheating the Christians. They're stealing the money from them. And in the middle of the studying, you know, in the yeshiva, they came, those, the Hungarian Hitler Jugend what they call. They used to call it Levente in Hungarian. And they jumped in through the door. And they started to chase everybody out. And I jumped out of the window. And they started to beat us up. And they chased us to go to see the movie. This is the first time I saw. Because we were not supposed to go to see movies. So I remembered they-- it was two kilometers they were chasing me. Some of the guys, they were able to run away. But I was caught in the middle with another three, four, yeshiva [INAUDIBLE], what they call them. And they chased us into the movie. They had to watch it. And they were make-- ridicule us. And they were beating us and laughing about us. And that's where the real problem started already. And then in [INAUDIBLE]-- of course I'm jumping back also-- when you are 12 years old, once a week, they had to take us for labor, hard labor, to-- also with the yellow star. And they used to beat the hell out from us. And they used to make us crawl in the mud kilometers just to punish us. That's the way every week. This was, it started already. Every day, you took the newspaper, a new law came out against the Jew. So it was already-- we knew already something was going to happen. But we didn't know what concentration camps. This, we didn't know. I thought only that they take the youngster-- the one who is older than, than 18-- they take him to the labor camp to work with the Germans, for the Hungarians. And the rest of the family will stay home and just going to leave us alone. But it didn't work out later like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at what age did you have to start wearing the yellow badge?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12. We had to go-- they used to call it the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Levente, they call it in Hungarian. That means to help the Hitler Jugend, the Hungarian Hitler Jugend. They used to-- the name was Levente, you know? You probably know what the Hitler Jugend. That was also the same thing. They were trained the same way like the Hitler Jugend. And they used to take us everywhere to, to hard labor. They used to take us in. I remember I was 12 years old, and they took us into the Christian school, the elementary school. And they made me and another friend carry those desk. They were for five students, you know, this long desk, very heavy. And they had to take it down from the second floor to the first floor. And the principal for the school, he was a lieutenant in the Hungarian army. And I was carrying it with my friend. It was very heavy. I couldn't carry it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, there was no place where to grab it. And it fell out from my hand. And it fell on the floor. So the guy beat the hell out from me, blood all over my face. Because he said that I'm sabotaging the Hungarian, you know? Do you remember your family making any attempts to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another story. It was my mother was always the initiative. I had an uncle who went to Israel, I think in 1934. And we were talking. I used to-- I remember listening to the story. My mother says, maybe we should leave. Because the British wouldn't let you in. You know the story. They gave only 1,500 certificates a year, if you remember. If I'm correct, you had to buy it. But we would be able to buy it because my uncle, he left with the three children to Israel. And he said that he has a way he can get the certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother used to bring it up. And my father says, how can we leave our grandfather here? And my grandfather wouldn't go before the Messiah comes to Israel. So we get stuck. My grandfather died, I think, in 1943. And the rest of the story you know. So when were you taken into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto, right away after Pesach-- approximately April 15th. I could be wrong a week, you know, because I don't exactly-- they let us finish the Pesach in, in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the atmosphere in the house that Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, terrible, terrible, terrible-- there was no bread. They couldn't-- we couldn't get matzos anymore. There was no flour. And they couldn't send us bread from [INAUDIBLE], because it was illegal. And in 1942, I think, they took away the mill from the family. Because they said-- even because they, in Hungary, they didn't bother the Jewish businesses. Or when the mill, they took away because they said a Jew not supposed to have such a big business. And they took it away. They were supposed to pay, not with money, with agriculture products.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they used to send bread for us. And after this, it was illegal. So would you believe it? I used to go on the field to pick up-- what do you call-- the wheat. And I used to take all the wheat in through a, a small mill to make little flours to bake the matzo for the last Pesach.

speaker: interviewee
text: But between, before, what happened about a year and a half when I came back from [INAUDIBLE], there was a family in Somotor. The name was [? Rabovsky, ?] not Jews. They, they liked my mother very much this family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were in the business of agriculture products-- you know, cotton, wheat, you know? They used to buy up from the farmers. And the wife of this [? Rabovsky ?] came to my mother-- let her rest in peace-- listen, I'm going to give you if you want flours. And of course, it's black market. So who will do it? My father will not do it. My brother, Betzalel [? Josef ?] will not do it. I decide I was always-- you know, I guess I was always born to do some kind of a business. I decided in the night, on the field, we used to-- the wheel barrow to bring 50 pounds of flours, bring it into the house. In the morning when I was going-- then I was coming home every day. I, I didn't sleep anymore in the town, you know, Satoraljaujhely.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home every evening. In the morning, I was taking the train. And I had-- I remember till today-- I had one package, I had seven kilo, in the other package, five kilo, 12 kilo. This is approximately 25 pounds. And I used to sell it on the black market for the bakeries-- for Jewish bakeries. And that's-- I used to make more money than an average.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is the end of the first tape.
